perturbation.original_text: Turpin hereby irrevocably and expressly waives as against any person all moral rights Turpin may have in any and all Work Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Turpin waives all moral rights Turpin may have in any and all Work Product.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'irrevocably' can be interpreted as making the waiver of moral rights revocable, which introduces uncertainty and could conflict with legal interpretations in jurisdictions where moral rights waivers must be explicit and permanent, increasing liability for the company.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Copyright Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-42) - Moral Rights
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-42, s. 14.1(4)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Canadian Copyright law, moral rights can be waived, but the waiver must be clear and unambiguous. While the altered clause still contains a waiver, the deletion of 'irrevocably' could lead to arguments that the waiver was not intended to be permanent, potentially contradicting the intent to transfer full rights to the company. This creates legal risk especially if the work is used in a way that infringes on the author's (Turpin's) moral rights (e.g., distortion of the work).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Copyright Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Moral rights 14.1 Â (1) Â The author of a work has, subject to section 28.2, the right to the integrity of the work and, in connection with an act mentioned in section 3, the right, where reasonable in the circumstances, to be associated with the work as its author by name or under a pseudonym and the right to remain anonymous. Marginal note: No assignment of moral rights (2) Â Moral rights may not be assigned but may be waived in whole or in part. Marginal note: No waiver by assignment (3) Â An assignment of copyright in a work does not by that act alone constitute a waiver of any moral rights. Marginal note: Effect of waiver (4) Â Where a waiver of any moral right is made in favour of an owner or a licensee of copyright, it may be invoked by any person authorized by the owner or licensee to use the work, unless there is an indication to the contrary in the waiver. perturbation.justification: The removal of 'irrevocably' from the moral rights waiver introduces ambiguity regarding the permanency of the waiver. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Marginal note: No assignment of moral rights '(2) Â Moral rights may not be assigned but may be waived in whole or in part.' and Marginal note: Effect of waiver '(4) Â Where a waiver of any moral right is made in favour of an owner or a licensee of copyright, it may be invoked by any person authorized by the owner or licensee to use the work, unless there is an indication to the contrary in the waiver', it can be argued that omitting 'irrevocably' weakens the clarity and enforceability of the waiver, which could be seen as undermining the company's rights, thus creating a legal contradiction and falling under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.
